title: deadlineHit failedTiles loop uses hardcoded level=0 instead of source.level
description: When a budget deadline is hit during progressive dc-then-final, lines 393-401 build failedTiles for tiles that were not stitched. The tileIdOf call at line 395 passes hardcoded 0 as the level argument, but the tile IDs built during Phase 1 and 2 (lines 242 and 325) use `source.level ?? 0`. If source.level is non-zero (which it is for non-zero pyramid levels via createLevelSource), the TileId.level values mismatch: stitchedFinal contains keys like 'L3-C0-R0' but the deadline loop generates 'L0-C0-R0', so no key is found in stitchedFinal and every tile is spuriously added to failedTiles, causing the caller to believe all tiles failed even when most succeeded.

title: dc-then-final pooled path fires final onTile for cache-hits before the final decode pass runs, reporting wrong completed counts
description: In decodeTiledViewportPooled's dc-then-final branch with cache hits (lines 1307-1343), the code fires 'final' stage onTile events for hit tiles (lines 1325-1329) with a prewarm-computed completed count BEFORE the actual final decodeTilesParallel pass runs. Then finOpts.progressBase is set to finalCompleted (which already includes these final onTile calls), and decodeTilesParallel fires MORE 'final' events for miss tiles. The consumer therefore sees final events out of order relative to when pixels are actually written: the 'final' paint notifications for hit tiles arrive before their counterparts for miss tiles have even started decoding. The total count reported by both final-pass events is also inflated for hit tiles (1+ hits reported before 1+ miss decodes begin).

title: convergedByteEnd == bytes is accepted but should be rejected as non-truncating
description: The validator at line 116 only fails when `cbe > bytes`, allowing cbe == bytes through as a valid value. However pickByteEndForQuality (manifest.ts line 137) gates on `pt.bytes < level.bytes` (strict less-than) and returns undefined for the equal case, meaning a convergedByteEnd equal to bytes is stored in the manifest but silently ignored at runtime. The validator should reject cbe >= bytes to make it clear that a value equal to total bytes is meaningless, preventing confusing no-op entries in manifests.

title: Cache quality key for dc-only mode uses 'final' cacheQuality, bypassing dc-keyed cache lookup on repeat calls
description: Line 160 computes `cacheQuality = progressive === 'dc-only' ? 'dc' : 'final'`. This is correct. However line 161 then builds `cacheKey = viewportCacheKey(getLevelId(source), vp, plan.format, cacheQuality)`, which is only used for the VIEWPORT-level cache (the whole-viewport entry). The tile-level DC cache inside the progressive branch (line 273, line 379) uses inline key strings `${makeTileCacheKey(levelId, id)}:${plan.format}:dc`. The viewport-level dc key is stored at line 418 via `cacheStore(cache, cacheKey, ...)`, but the subsequent cache lookup at lines 162-175 checks `cacheKey` (now a dc-qualified key) only for dc-only. If a caller previously stored under the dc key, the next call with progressive='dc-only' will correctly hit. However, the test `dc-only progressive mode emits only DC tile passes and caches under qdc` at decode-level.test.ts:379 checks `viewportCacheKey(..., 'dc')` which matches cacheQuality='dc'. The actual bug is subtle: `cacheQuality` is computed on line 160 before the parallelEligible delegation check (line 137-142). When `parallelEligible=true` this function returns early to `decodeTiledViewportPooled`, but `cacheQuality` is never used to pass dc vs final quality to the pooled path. The pooled path at tiled-decode-pool.ts:1119 always computes `cacheKeyFinal` with hardcoded 'final' quality, so dc-only progressive in the pooled path always caches and retrieves under 'final', breaking the viewport cache for dc-only mode.

title: JXTC safety cap uses 1<<30 integer shift which is 0 in JS for large values due to 32-bit sign
description: Line 64 checks `totalBytes > (1 << 30)` and `imageW > (1 << 24)`. In JavaScript, `1 << 30` is 1073741824 (positive, correct for i32). However `1 << 31` would be negative (-2147483648) due to signed 32-bit shift semantics. The current value of `1 << 30` (~1 GiB) is fine as-is, but documenting this fragility is important: if the threshold were ever changed to `1 << 31` or higher it would silently allow oversized images through. The current code is correct but the comment saying 'safe total byte size cap ~1GB (2^30)' is accurate — no functional bug today.

title: decodeTilesParallel total progress count is halved for dc-only progressive mode
description: Line 926 computes `total = opts.progressTotal ?? (tiles.length * (opts.progressiveStage ? 2 : 1))`. The intent is that when a progressiveStage is provided, the total accounts for both DC and final passes (× 2). But dc-only progressive only runs a single pass (stage='dc') — there is no subsequent 'final' call. When decode-level.ts calls the DC-only direct path, it passes `total = n` (line 207, where `progressive === 'dc-only' ? n : n * 2`). However when the pooled path calls decodeTilesParallel for a dc-only scenario with progressiveStage='dc', the formula gives `tiles.length * 2` (because opts.progressiveStage='dc' is truthy), making completed/total show 1/2, 2/2... instead of 1/1, 2/1. The caller must explicitly pass progressTotal to avoid this, but dc-only mode never does so in the current pooled path.

title: Handle state transition table allows WarmFloor -> WarmFloor self-transition to pass in DEV mode
description: The transition check at line 64 is `if (DEV && !allowed[cur]?.includes(next) && cur !== next)`. The `cur !== next` guard means any self-transition (same state to same state) silently passes in DEV mode without updating state or logging. This is intentional for idempotency, but it also means calling setHandleState(h, HandleState.Active) on an already-Active handle does nothing and does not throw, masking potential double-activation bugs in higher-level logic (e.g., acquire calling setHandleState twice on a newly-spawned handle).

title: armAllExcessIdle iterates in LIFO (hottest) order but reaping should prefer FIFO (coldest)
description: armAllExcessIdle at lines 590-593 iterates from `idle.length - 1` down to `minIdle`, arming timers on the hottest (most recently used) idle handles first. This is inverted: the hottest handles are most likely to be reused soon and should be kept; the coldest (earliest in the idle array) should be reaped first. The LIFO push in release (line 550) means `idle[0]` is coldest and `idle[idle.length-1]` is hottest. Arming a short timer on the hottest handle while leaving coldest alone increases churn for the most cache-warm workers.

title: decodeWhole viewport cache uses getLevelId(bytes) as ID but bytes is a Uint8Array, while tiled path uses getLevelId(source)
description: decodeWhole at line 563 builds the cache key as `getLevelId(bytes)` where `bytes` is the raw Uint8Array passed as the first argument. In decodeLevel, the bytes come from `source.bytes` (a whole-source LevelSource). If the same LevelSource is passed to decodeLevel twice, the whole-path cache key will be stable (same buffer identity). However, if a caller constructs a whole LevelSource with the same bytes buffer but a different LevelSource object and passes the raw bytes, getLevelId(bytes) and getLevelId(source) produce different IDs (one uses bytesId, one uses the LevelSource WeakMap). This means cache warm-up via one call path doesn't help the other. The tiled path consistently uses getLevelId(source), so the whole path should also use getLevelId(nominalSource ?? bytes) for consistency.

title: Opportunity: validateLevel does not verify that w, h, bytes are positive integers
description: requireNumber only checks that values are finite numbers; it does not reject w=0, h=-1, or bytes=0. A manifest with w=0 or h=0 would pass validation and then cause divide-by-zero or zero-allocation downstream in createLevelSource (which does check but throws a generic OOM/BAD_MANIFEST). Adding explicit positivity checks in validateLevel with clear error messages would give callers earlier and more descriptive failures.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled outBuffer cache-hit path checks cached.length <= ob.byteLength but never checks cached.length >= need
description: The cache-hit outBuffer path at lines 1126-1129 guards with `cached.length >= need && cached.length <= ob.byteLength`, but if cached.length > ob.byteLength (which cannot happen given the need <= ob.byteLength guard above), the hit falls through silently to a full decode. The real issue is the condition `cached.length >= need && cached.length <= ob.byteLength` — if the outBuffer happens to be exactly `need` bytes, then `cached.length <= ob.byteLength` is equivalent to `cached.length <= need`, so any cached value larger than need (e.g. from a prior larger viewport) would fall through to a full redecode. This is inconsistent with the non-outBuffer path which only checks `cached.length >= need`. The second guard should be `cached.length <= ob.byteLength` (correct) but the combination still allows cached mismatches for oversized caches.

title: makeTiledSource helper omits tilesX/tilesY from the returned LevelSource object
description: The LevelSource 'tiled' type requires tilesX and tilesY fields (per level-source.ts line 10). makeTiledSource at lines 102-124 in the test creates the JXTC header bytes with tilesX=2, tilesY=1, but the returned object literal (line 113-123) does not include tilesX or tilesY fields. TypeScript may catch this as a type error at compile time, but if the test file runs with type assertions bypassed (the cast to LevelSource is implicit), the missing fields could cause downstream callers that read source.tilesX to get undefined instead of 2.

title: complete condition uses && skipTiles.size > 0 but should use || to detect any incompleteness
description: Line 416: `const complete = !deadlineHit && failedTiles.length === 0 && !(skipTiles && skipTiles.size > 0)`. The last clause `!(skipTiles && skipTiles.size > 0)` correctly prevents caching under 'final' when some tiles were skipped. However the condition works correctly as-is; this is not a bug. Confirming: the semantics are 'complete iff no deadline, no fails, no skips' — all three conditions are needed with AND. No bug here.

title: Opportunity: tilesOverlappingRegion property test does not verify full coverage (sum of tile areas == region area for aligned cases)
description: The random property test at lines 72-96 verifies individual tile bounds are within the region but does not assert that the union of all tiles covers the entire region (no gaps). A tiling bug that produces only a subset of required tiles would pass this test. A coverage assertion (e.g., that each pixel of the region belongs to at least one tile) would catch off-by-one errors in txMax/tyMax calculations. The fast-check based choose-level test has a similar gap: it verifies the fallback but not the exact optimality invariant.
